input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Tooling and enablers include Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for handling system complexity across abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, and physical). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) like ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs are used to model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior is described via MontiArc Automaton or SysML v1.7 state machines, with detailed simulation using MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Sensor data is streamed via MQTT protocol, providing live measurements as stated in the abstract and Section IV.A. Specific synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, and temporal granularity details are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system architecture is defined by the Digital Shadow Reference Model as a UML class diagram, with a Processing component that consumes and produces data traces to orchestrate component integration and resource management.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems uses MQTT for data streaming, Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized interoperability, and interfaces with the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller for industrial control systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) utilizing SysML for multi-layered system modeling across requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers. Structural components are described via Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs), capturing components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral aspects are modeled using MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, while analytical simulations leverage MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java). Data originates from physical system state traces, managed through aggregation and abstraction processes for specific purposes, with integration of heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives across diverse modeling tools.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical system employs a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis, with a gear transmission consisting of a cycloidal gear stage. Control inputs include position, speed, and acceleration parameters for each axis, allowing independent actuation with precise motion control capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure comprises two position encoders per drive, with motor encoders mounted directly on the motor shaft. Sensor data streams, including input current and output angle measurements, are transmitted via MQTT protocol in real-time time-series format.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: One-way data flow from physical to digital via MQTT protocol, transmitting time series sensor data including target and actual position values, derivatives of position, and motor current measurements. No details on data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, or event triggers are provided.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: No virtual-to-physical interaction occurs; the digital shadow functions as a passive entity that neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Data transmission uses MQTT protocol for sensor streaming. Network topology, bandwidth requirements, latency specifications, reliability mechanisms, redundancy, and security protocols are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin development process employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT to manage system complexity across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs are utilized for component & connector perspectives, while behavior modeling is supported by tools like MathWorks Simulink, simulation frameworks, and general-purpose programming languages such as C++ and Java.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with SysML, AADL, ACME, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for architecture description and modeling.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Data is streamed via MQTT protocol, and a physical safety cell restricts the robot's working range to ensure operational safety.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction states the characteristic is 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes the physical system in Section III 'USE CASE'. It identifies the system as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and details its components, such as the 'first vertical robot axis... driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor' and 'gear transmission', as well as its control via 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'.

The extraction states the characteristic is 'Not in Document', but the source text describes specific services provided by the digital shadow/twin. The Abstract mentions the methodology includes 'visualization in a dashboard', and the Introduction states the digital shadow helps 'enhance our understanding of joint friction and ultimately improve robot motion accuracy'. Section IV.A also identifies the purpose as 'understanding of the relations and detection of friction loss'. These correspond to visualization, monitoring, and optimization services.

The description is highly detailed and accurately reflects Section II.A of the source text, listing specific MBSE methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT), ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs), and simulation tools (MathWorks Simulink, C++, Java).

block_2_judge.reasoning: Accurately describes the data streaming method using MQTT for live measurements and correctly identifies that specific temporal details such as synchronization frequencies and latency are absent from the text. Supported by: 'time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT' and 'live measurements'.

The paper focuses on a single digital shadow for the first axis of a specific robot. It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures of twins, or coordination mechanisms between different twins. Therefore, the extraction correctly identifies that this information is not in the document.

The characteristic accurately describes the Digital Shadow Reference Model as a UML class diagram, with Section II.B stating 'Fig. 1 presents this refined version of the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) as a Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagram.' It correctly identifies the Processing component that 'both consumes and produces data traces.' However, the claim about this component being used to 'orchestrate component integration and resource management' goes beyond what's explicitly stated in the text. The text describes the Processing component's role in data processing, not in orchestrating component integration.

The characteristic correctly identifies the technologies mentioned in the text: MQTT for data streaming (Section IV.A states 'streamed via MQTT'), Asset Administration Shells (Abstract mentions 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)'), and the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller (Section III states 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'). However, the claim about these being used for 'integration with external systems' and 'standardized interoperability' goes beyond what's directly stated. The paper focuses on creating a digital shadow for a single robot, not necessarily integration with external systems beyond the robot's own controller.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The provided text discusses digital shadows, modeling, and data traceability (e.g., 'data remains fully traceable to its origin'), but it does not contain any information regarding data ownership policies, privacy protection, consent management, or data sharing agreements. Therefore, the statement 'Not in Document' is accurate.

The extraction accurately cites two specific safety measures. First, regarding physical safety, the text states: 'The safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range to [-90°, 90°]' (Section III). Second, regarding data transmission, the text mentions: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT' (Section IV). These quotes support the extracted description.
